verbatim: And as for the splendid sum of gold which you gave for my journey home, Baccheius, the bearer of this letter, is taking it back to you. For it was neither a sufficient sum for my journey nor was it otherwise useful for my support; and since it reflects the greatest disgrace on you who offer it, and not much less on me if I accept it, I therefore refuse to accept it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific social expectation and practice where a ruler (Dionysius) provides financial resources for the travel and support of a high-status associate or philosopher (Plato). Plato's rejection stems from the amount being insufficient, implying a norm where such support is expected to be commensurate with the recipient's status.

verbatim: I hear from Archedemus... that you think that not only I myself should keep quiet but my friends also from doing or saying anything bad about you... Now your saying this, that Dion is excepted, implies that I have no control over my friends; for had I had this control over you and Dion, as well as the rest, more blessings would have come to us all.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Dionysius expects Plato to exercise authority over his 'friends' or disciples to prevent them from speaking ill of the tyrant. Plato discusses this expectation as a form of social 'control' (or lack thereof) over the group of associates, defining a norm where an intellectual leader is held responsible for the behaviors and speech of his group members.

verbatim: But if you are contented with my doctrines, then you should hold me also in special honor. So now, just as at the beginning, do you lead the way and I will follow. If I am honored by you, I will honor you; but if I am not honored I will keep to myself. Moreover, if you honor me and take the lead in so doing, you will be thought to be honoring philosophy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato outlines a reciprocal rule of social exchange based on education/expertise: a ruler who accepts the doctrines of a philosopher is expected to grant that philosopher public honor. This honor is explicitly linked to the status of the discipline itself.

verbatim: for I saw then, as I see now, that, as a rule, when great and exorbitant wealth is in the hands either of private citizens or of monarchs, the greater it is, the greater and more numerous are the slanderers it breeds and the hordes of parasites and wastrels—than which there is no greater evil generated by wealth or by the other privileges of power.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a social regularity or 'rule' where the possession of great wealth (whether by private citizens or monarchs) mechanically leads to an increase in social professional hazards like slander and parasites, reducing the individual's security and peace.

verbatim: Next, I requested that Dion’s family should have possession of his property, instead of the distributors, whom you wot of, having the distribution of it. And further, I deemed it right that the revenue which was usually paid over to him year by year should be forwarded to him all the more, rather than all the less, because of my presence. None of these requests being granted, I asked leave to depart.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the situation of Dion, a high-ranking associate of the tyrant. While Plato argues for the right of the exile's family to keep the property and for revenues to be sent to the exile, the actual practice (the rule in the Syracusan tyranny) was to seize and redistribute the property of those cast out.

verbatim: Many thanks for releasing the man in the stone-quarries; and my request with regard to his household and Hegesippus, the son of Ariston, is no hard matter; for in your letter you said that should anyone wrong him or them and you come to know of it you would not allow it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Building on the specific instance of a man being released from the stone quarries (a place of penal servitude) and the promise of protection for his household, this reflects a rule or norm where the ruler intervenes to prevent wrongful detention or 'wronging' of certain individuals and their households.

verbatim: For the man is useful for many things, the most important being that in which you yourself are deficient owing to your youth, and also because it is a matter about which there are not many counsellors available for the young. For forms of government, like animals, have each their own kind of language, one for democracy, another for oligarchy, and a third kind for monarchy; and though a vast number of people would assert that they understand these languages, yet all but a few of them are very far indeed from discerning them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato notes that the king (Perdiccas) is deficient in the 'science of government' specifically due to his youth, implying that young rulers are systematically considered to lack the necessary knowledge (the 'language' of polities) and require older experts (like Euphraeus) to interpret it for them.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to speak in the demos
verbatim: Suppose, however, that on hearing this someone were to say: Plato, as it seems, is claiming to know what is of advantage to democracy; yet when he has had it in his power to speak before the demos and to counsel it for the best he has never yet stood up and made a speech—to this you may reply that Plato was born late in the history of his country, and he found the demos already old and habituated by the previous statesmen to do many things at variance with his own counsel.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text acknowledges that as a citizen, Plato had the 'power to speak before the demos' (the assembly of citizens). While Plato explains why he chose not to use it, the passage confirms that the legal/social structure provided citizens this specific political resource.

verbatim: And indeed I saw how these men within a short time caused men to look back on the former government as a golden age; and above all how they treated my aged friend Socrates... when they tried to send him, along with others, after one of the citizens, to fetch him by force that he might be put to death—their object being that Socrates, whether he wished or no, might be made to share in their political actions; he, however, refused to obey and risked the uttermost penalties rather than be a partaker in their unholy deeds.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes the illegal actions of the 'Thirty' (a specific historical revolutionary government) as 'unholy' and 'evil practices' because they attempted to seize and kill a citizen without following established legal norms. This demonstrates that under the regular Athenian constitution, citizens possessed the resource of protection from arbitrary execution.

verbatim: But, as ill-luck would have it, certain men of authority summoned our comrade Socrates before the law-courts, laying a charge against him which was most unholy, and which Socrates of all men least deserved; for it was on the charge of impiety that those men summoned him and the rest condemned and slew him
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato notes that even in a 'troublous' time of restoration, the procedure for dealing with a citizen (Socrates) involved summoning him before law-courts. While he objects to the verdict, the process reflects the institutional rule that citizens are entitled to a court proceeding rather than summary dismissal or extrajudicial killing.

verbatim: Wherefore the classes of mankind (I said) will have no cessation from evils until either the class of those who are right and true philosophers attains political supremacy, or else the class of those who hold power in the States becomes, by some dispensation of Heaven, really philosophic.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato identifies 'true philosophers' as a distinct group and explicitly states that they currently lack 'political supremacy' in all existing states. He presents this as a lack of access to a specific immaterial resource (power) under current societal rules.

verbatim: To a slave, however, I would give advice, and if he refused it I would use compulsion.  But to a father or mother I deem it impious to apply compulsion, unless they are in the grip of the disease of insanity;  but if they are living a settled life which is pleasing to them, though not to me, I would neither irritate them with vain exhortations nor yet minister to them with flatteries by providing them with means to satisfy appetites of a sort such that I, were I addicted to them, would refuse to live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato contrasts the treatment of free parents with that of slaves regarding personal advice and guidance; while parents cannot be forced, he explicitly states that a slave should be compelled if they refuse advice, indicating that slaves lacked the resource of personal autonomy and protection from physical or forceful correction in their conduct.

verbatim: Now those who have gained the mastery, whenever they become desirous of safety, ought always to choose out among themselves such men of Greek origin as they know by inquiry to be most excellent—men who are, in the first place, old, and who have wives and children at home, and forefathers as numerous and good and famous as possible, and who are all in the possession of ample property;  and for a city of ten thousand citizens, fifty such men would be a sufficient number These men they should fetch from their homes by means of entreaties and the greatest possible honors;  and when they have fetched them they should entreat and enjoin them to frame laws, under oath that they will give no advantage either to conquerors or conquered, but equal rights in common to the whole city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato outlines specific criteria for those who should be selected to frame laws in a state post-conflict. The criteria (age, distinguished lineage, and significant wealth) define a specific group that is granted the resource of high political authority and the right to draft a constitution.

verbatim: but if his nature is bad—and, in fact, the condition of most men’s souls in respect of learning and of what are termed morals is either naturally bad or else corrupted,—then not even Lynceus himself could make such folk see. In one word, neither receptivity nor memory will ever produce knowledge in him who has no affinity with the object, since it does not germinate to start with in alien states of mind; consequently neither those who have no natural connection or affinity with things just, and all else that is fair, although they are both receptive and retentive in various ways of other things, nor yet those who possess such affinity but are unreceptive and unretentive—none, I say, of these will ever learn to the utmost possible extent the truth of virtue nor yet of vice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a general rule of human development in his society: that the resource of 'knowledge' or 'truth of virtue' is fundamentally inaccessible to those whose 'nature' (soul's condition) is bad or corrupted, regardless of effort, because knowledge requires a natural affinity or health of the soul.

verbatim: It happened next, after no long interval, that whereas Dionysius had previously allowed Dion to remain in possession of his own property and to enjoy the income, he now ceased to permit Dion’s trustees to remit it to the Peloponnese, just as though he had entirely forgotten the terms of his letter, claiming that the property belonged not to Dion but to his son, his own nephew, of whom he was the legal trustee.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a legal situation in Syracuse where Dionysius, acting as the legal trustee for his nephew (a minor), asserts control over the property. This reflects the standard legal norm that minors do not have the right to manage their own property or income, which is instead held and administered by a legal trustee/guardian.

verbatim: And all the property he shall take shall be deposited in the Peloponnese and Athens with such persons as you shall think fit; and he shall enjoy the income from it but shall not be authorized to remove it without your consent. For I do not altogether trust him to act justly towards me if he had the use of these funds—for they will be by no means small; and I put more trust in you and your friends.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Dionysius proposes a rule for Dion (who is residing abroad as a non-resident/exile) where his property must be held by trustees and he is barred from moving the principal without their consent. In the context of Greek law, exiles or those living abroad often faced restrictions on liquidating or moving local assets without local guarantors or trustees.

verbatim: Now Dionysius attempted, contrary to his father’s practice, to reduce the pay of the older members of his mercenary force, and the soldiers, being infuriated, assembled together and refused to permit it. And when he kept trying to force them by closing the gates of the citadel, they immediately rushed up to the walls shouting out a kind of barbaric war-chant; whereupon Dionysius became terribly alarmed and conceded all and even more than all to those of the peltasts that were then assembled.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific instance where the ruler attempted to reduce pay, but the group of mercenaries collective action forced a return to the established 'practice' of his predecessor. This establishes a norm where mercenaries were entitled to their agreed-upon wages without arbitrary reduction.

verbatim: I sent to Archytas and my other friends in Tarentum stating the position in which I found myself: and they, having found some pretext for an Embassy from the State, dispatched a thirty-oared vessel, and with it one of themselves, called Lamiscus; and he, when he came, made request to Dionysius concerning me, saying that I was desirous to depart, and begging him by all means to give his consent. To this he agreed, and he sent me forth after giving me supplies for the journey
and he, though he probably believed that I, as many slanderers asserted, was conspiring with you against himself and his throne, yet refrained from killing me, and showed compunction.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato, an Athenian citizen and guest, describes how the intervention of a foreign embassy (Tarentum) secured his safe departure from Syracuse even when suspected of conspiracy. This illustrates the rule of protection for high-status foreign guests/philosophers through diplomatic pressure.

verbatim: if I prove able to fetch Heracleides here to answer the charges now made against him, in case it is decided that he must not reside in Sicily, I claim that he should have a passage to the Peloponnese, taking his son and his wife, and reside there without doing injury to Dionysius, and enjoying the income from his property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the negotiation regarding Heracleides, it is established as a formal condition of exile (rather than total confiscation) that he should be allowed to receive the income from his estates while living abroad.

verbatim: he sent me out of the citadel, inventing the excuse that the women had to perform a sacrifice of ten days’ duration in the garden where I was lodging; so during this period he gave orders that I should stay outside with Archedemus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage highlights a societal rule where religious sacrifices performed by women required the total exclusion of men from the specific garden/lodging space for the duration of the rites.

verbatim: and in addition to these there should be judges selected from the magistrates of each preceding year, one from each magistracy—the one, that is, who is approved as the most good and just; and these should decide for the ensuing year all cases which involve the death, imprisonment or transportation of citizens;
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato is outlining a legal constitution for contemporary Syracuse. He specifies that a subset of judges for capital cases must be drawn from the pool of individuals who served as magistrates in the previous year, granting this group exclusive access to these specific judicial roles.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from ritual pollution of blood
verbatim: and it should not be permissible for a king to be a judge of such suits, but he, like a priest, should remain clean from bloodshed and imprisonment and exile.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In his constitutional proposal, Plato mandates that the kings (a specific noble group) are barred from judging capital crimes. This is framed as a resource: the protection of their ritual purity (staying 'clean' like a priest), which is a specific status-requirement for their role.

verbatim: And as controllers of war and peace they should appoint Law-wardens, thirty-five in number, in conjunction with the People and the Council.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes the governance of the state where the power to appoint Law-wardens (who control war and peace) is shared with 'the People', granting the citizen body an immaterial right to participate in high-level appointments.

verbatim: And these men, on their arrival, should first lay down laws and a constitution which is so framed as to permit the kings to be put in control of the temples and of all else that fitly belongs to those who once were benefactors.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato proposes that the new constitution grant the group of kings (based on lineage/nobility) specific control over the temples as a right belonging to their status as descendants of benefactors.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to attend to private business
verbatim: Now it is plain to almost everyone that the pleasantest thing in life is to attend to one’s own business, especially when the business one chooses is such as yours; yet you ought also to bear in mind that no one of us exists for himself alone, but one share of our existence belongs to our country, another to our parents, a third to the rest of our friends, while a great part is given over to those needs of the hour with which our life is beset. And when our country itself calls us to public duties, it were surely improper not to hearken to the call; for to do so will involve the further consequence of leaving room to worthless men who engage in public affairs from motives that are by no means the best.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a normative expectation in Greek city-states (like Tarentum or Athens) where a citizen's right to prioritize private life is subordinate to the state's claim on their time for public office/duty.

verbatim: For they are mistaken if they believe that a constitution could ever be well established by any kind of legislation whatsoever without the existence of some authority in the State which supervises the daily life both of slaves and freemen, to see that it is both temperate and manly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato asserts that a well-ordered state requires an authority to monitor and regulate the daily conduct of all inhabitants, including slaves, to ensure 'temperate and manly' behavior.

verbatim: In the next place, as regards the spending of your own money at Athens, I have to tell you, first of all, that, contrary to what we supposed, you have not a single friend who will advance money in case I am required to spend something on furnishing a chorus or the like; and further, if you yourself have some urgent affair on hand in which prompt expenditure is to your advantage, whereas it is to your disadvantage to have the expenditure deferred until the arrival of a messenger from you, such a state of affairs is not only awkward but reflects also on your honor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text refers to the 'furnishing a chorus' (choregia), a standard liturgy in Athens where wealthy individuals/families were legally or socially required to fund public festivals.

verbatim: I have in my charge four daughters of those nieces of mine who died at the time when you bade me to wear a crown, and I refused; and of these one is of marriageable age, one eight years old, one a little over three years, and the fourth not yet a year old. To these girls I and my friends must give portions—to all of them, that is, whom I live to see married; as to the rest, they must look to themselves. . . Now the eldest one is marrying Speusippus, she being his sister’s daughter. So for her I require no more than thirty minas, that being for us a reasonable dowry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The verbatim outlines the standard social rule that female relatives (nieces/daughters) are entitled to a dowry (portion) provided by their male guardians to facilitate marriage.

extracted_rules.rule: Education of young men in philosophy
verbatim: Keep well and study philosophy and exhort thereto all the other young men; and greet for me your comrades at the game of ball The Greek word fellow-spherists suggests a play on the double meaning of sphere as ball and globe (cf. Plat. L. 2.312d); so that the real meaning may be fellow-astronomers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato explicitly encourages the ruler to exhort 'all the other young men' to study philosophy. This identifies young men as the specific social group targeted for the resource of education/philosophical training in this contemporary setting.
